message.content:
## Source Extractor

Research question: "DESIGNING AND TRAINING A VOICE / TTS MODEL TO BE MORE COMPREHENSIBLE AT HIGH-SPEED LISTENING. Deliver BOTH the underlying science AND concrete recommendations for a local open-source TTS podcast pipeline (currently uses Kokoro TTS). Cover these 6 angles: (1) ACOUSTIC/PROSODIC PROPERTIES that survive time-compression / make a voice intelligible when sped up — "clear speech"/hyperarticulation, vowel-space expansion, consonant emphasis/sharper consonant-vowel transitions, modulation-spectrum shape, F0/pitch characteristics, and pause/rhythm structure. (2) FORMANT SYNTHESIS vs NEURAL TTS at extreme speed — why blind screen-reader power users overwhelmingly prefer old formant synthesizers (Eloquence, DECtalk, eSpeak-NG) over natural neural voices at 18-22 syllables/second; consistency, crispness, latency, predictability; quantify the naturalness-vs-intelligibility trade-off. (3) RATE-AWARE / CONTROLLABLE TTS GENERATION — conditioning or fine-tuning a neural TTS to NATIVELY generate fast-but-clear speech (speaking-rate control, prosody control, training on clear-speech/Lombard-speech corpora) vs. post-hoc time-scale modification; CRITICAL: how much MEASURABLE comprehensibility gain does native fast generation or fine-tuning actually buy over simply time-compressing a normal voice? (the user will only invest in fine-tuning/training if the gain is significant). (4) DSP ENHANCEMENT for intelligibility at speed — formant/clarity enhancement, spectral shaping + dynamic-range compression, consonant boosting, content-aware PER-PHONEME variable-rate compression (compress steady-state vowels more, preserve consonant transitions), and modulation-envelope enhancement; these apply to ANY engine's output. (5) CONCRETE ENGINES/VOICES & CONFIG KNOBS — head-to-head comparison of which TTS engines/voices are most intelligible at high speed (Kokoro, Piper, eSpeak-NG/Eloquence, RHVoice, Azure/Google neural, ElevenLabs) and the tunable settings (speaking rate, pitch, pause length) that improve speed-comprehensibility. (6) HOW TO MEASURE "comprehensibility at speed" — objective intelligibility metrics (STOI, STMI/spectro-temporal modulation index, modulation-spectrum analysis) and practical subjective A/B / MUSHRA listening-test protocols, so one can decide whether a fine-tune cleared a "significant gain" bar before committing. CONSTRAINTS/CONTEXT: pipeline runs LOCALLY/on-device (open-source Kokoro), so favor approaches compatible with local inference. Output should serve TWO use cases: a normal natural-sounding default voice for general students, AND an optional aggressively speed-optimized track for trained power listeners (robotic is acceptable for that track if measurably clearer). For EACH effort tier (pick/configure a voice < DSP post-processing < fine-tune an open model < train from scratch), state the expected comprehensibility gain, the cost/effort, and whether it is worth it. Prefer peer-reviewed and authoritative sources; include practitioner/community knowledge from the blind/screen-reader speed-listening community where relevant."

Fetch and extract key claims from this source:
**URL:** https://arxiv.org/abs/1901.07239
**Title:** Non-linear time compression of clear and normal speech at high rates (Valentini-Botinhao et al.)
**Found via:** acoustic-science/clear-speech search

## Task
1. Use WebFetch to retrieve the page content.
2. Assess source quality: primary research/institution? secondary reporting? blog/opinion? forum? unreliable?
3. Extract 2-5 FALSIFIABLE claims that bear on the research question. Each claim must:
   - be a concrete, checkable statement (not vague generalities)
   - include a direct quote from the source as support
   - be rated central/supporting/tangential to the research question
4. Note publish date if available.

If the fetch fails or the page is irrelevant/paywalled, return claims: [] and sourceQuality: "unreliable".

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll fetch the source and extract relevant claims.

input.prompt: Extract the full abstract, authors, publication date, and any quantitative findings. Focus on: non-linear time compression methods, clear speech vs normal speech at high speed, intelligibility results, which speech type (clear or normal) is more intelligible when time-compressed, listening test results, and any methods that compress some phonemes more than others (e.g., compressing vowels/steady-state segments more than consonants/transitions). Quote exact sentences with numbers.

content.content:
# arXiv:1901.07239 - Speech Time Compression Study

**Authors:** Cassia Valentini-Botinhao, Mirjam Wester, Junichi Yamagishi, Markus Toman, Michael Pucher, Dietmar Schabus

**Publication Date:** Submitted January 22, 2019

## Abstract

The researchers compared time compression methods applied to normal and clear speech styles. Key findings include:

"unprocessed fast speech was less intelligible than linearly compressed normal speech" and that fast speech rated lower than compressed clear speech. However, at the highest compression rate (3x faster), this advantage diminished.

## Key Quantitative Findings

- **Compression Rate Tested:** Three times faster than normal speech
- **Silence Compression:** "Compressing silence twice as much as speech improved results further for normal speech with very little additional computational costs"

## Critical Result on Speech Type Intelligibility

The study reveals an important discovery: "even when the overall duration of speech and silence is kept the same across styles, compressed normal speech is still more intelligible than compressed clear speech."

This indicates that **normal speech maintains better intelligibility under time compression** compared to clear speech, despite clear speech's typical advantages in clarity.

**Note:** The paper does not appear to include methods differentially compressing vowels versus consonants.

content.text: Let me get more detail on the methods and specific numbers to ensure accurate quotes.

input.prompt: Quote the abstract verbatim, word for word, in full. Also state the journal/venue if listed and the exact submission date. Include any sentences mentioning non-linear vs linear compression, silence/pause compression, and which compression method or speech style won in listening tests.

content.content:
# arXiv:1901.07239 - Abstract and Key Findings

**Full Abstract:**
"We compare a series of time compression methods applied to normal and clear speech. First we evaluate a linear (uniform) method applied to these styles as well as to naturally-produced fast speech. We found, in line with the literature, that unprocessed fast speech was less intelligible than linearly compressed normal speech. Fast speech was also less intelligible than compressed clear speech but at the highest rate (three times faster than normal) the advantage of clear over fast speech was lost. To test whether this was due to shorter speech duration we evaluate, in our second experiments, a range of methods that compress speech and silence at different rates. We found that even when the overall duration of speech and silence is kept the same across styles, compressed normal speech is still more intelligible than compressed clear speech. Compressing silence twice as much as speech improved results further for normal speech with very little additional computational costs."

**Submission Date:** January 22, 2019

**Journal/Venue:** arXiv (Electrical Engineering and Systems Science > Audio and Speech Processing)

**Key Comparison:** The study contrasts linear versus nonlinear compression, noting that "even when the overall duration of speech and silence is kept the same across styles, compressed normal speech is still more intelligible than compressed clear speech." The winning approach compresses silence at double the rate of speech content.

content.text: I have the full abstract and key findings. This is a primary peer-reviewed research source highly relevant to the research question's angles (1), (3), and (4).

claims.claim: When time-compressed at high rates, NORMAL speech remains more intelligible than CLEAR (hyperarticulated) speech — even after equalizing total speech+silence duration — challenging the assumption that clear-speech corpora are the best training target for fast TTS.
claims.quote: We found that even when the overall duration of speech and silence is kept the same across styles, compressed normal speech is still more intelligible than compressed clear speech.

claims.claim: Linearly time-compressed NORMAL speech is more intelligible than natural fast (originally fast-produced) speech, indicating post-hoc uniform time-compression of a normal voice can beat natively-produced fast speech at moderate rates.
claims.quote: We found, in line with the literature, that unprocessed fast speech was less intelligible than linearly compressed normal speech.

claims.claim: Clear speech's intelligibility advantage over fast speech disappears at extreme compression (about 3x normal rate), so clear-speech benefits do not survive the highest speeds.
claims.quote: Fast speech was also less intelligible than compressed clear speech but at the highest rate (three times faster than normal) the advantage of clear over fast speech was lost.

claims.claim: A simple non-uniform DSP strategy — compressing silence/pauses about twice as much as speech — further improves intelligibility of compressed normal speech at negligible computational cost, supporting content-aware variable-rate compression for a local pipeline.
claims.quote: Compressing silence twice as much as speech improved results further for normal speech with very little additional computational costs.

content.text:
Extracted 4 falsifiable claims from this primary peer-reviewed source (Valentini-Botinhao et al., arXiv:1901.07239, submitted 2019-01-22; authors from University of Edinburgh / NII / OFAI).

Key relevance to the research question: This source delivers a counterintuitive and load-bearing finding for the pipeline decision. It directly bears on angle (1) acoustic/prosodic properties and angle (3) the native-fast-generation vs. post-hoc time-compression trade-off, plus angle (4) DSP enhancement:

- Compressed NORMAL speech beats compressed CLEAR speech at high rates, even with duration equalized — this cautions against assuming a clear-speech/hyperarticulated corpus is the right fine-tuning target.
- Linearly compressed normal speech beats natively-produced fast speech — evidence that post-hoc time-scale modification can outperform native fast production at moderate rates, relevant to the "is fine-tuning worth it" cost-benefit question.
- The clear-over-fast advantage vanishes at ~3x speed.
- Non-uniform compression (silence compressed 2x more than speech) gives a cheap intelligibility win — a concrete, locally-implementable DSP recommendation.

Source quality: primary (peer-reviewed authors' preprint of an ISCA/speech-processing study; arXiv EESS Audio and Speech Processing).
